reference: What brings you in? I've just come in today because I've been having some issues with my hips as well as my knees. OK, and you said it started in the past month? Yeah, maybe up to two months actually. Yeah, I would say two months. OK, and is the pain and stiffness always there or does it come and go? It's, it comes and goes. It's worse in the, so it's stiff, more stiff in the morning first, but like as the day goes on, probably near the afternoon it gets worse. So I've had to stop some of my activities like gardening and being outdoors and things like that. OK. And you said it's, it gets worse with activity. Is there anything else that makes it better or worse? Uhm, it probably, yeah, if I'm just exerting myself too much. Uh, in terms of better, I I think I've taken some Advil that does help with the pain. OK, OK. And then if you were to describe the quality of this pain, would you say it's achy, throbbing, dull, or sharp? It's kind of like this uh, achy pain, kind of deep within the joints themselves. OK. Yeah, it just gets worse. I, as I walk more and do more activities. OK, and does the pain radiate also or does it, or is it pretty localized to your hips and knees? It's mainly in those areas. OK. And severity wise, one to 10, 10 being the worst pain you've ever felt, how bad is this pain? Probably like a 7. Seven, OK. And have you ever experienced this type of pain before? Uh, no, not really. And do you think your pain is getting worse? Uhm, I think so. Like over the last two months, I feel like my pain, there's more stiffness, there's more pain throughout the day. OK, OK. And yeah, so do you, are you experiencing any sort of numbness or tingling in the areas? No, not really. No, OK. Any muscle weakness? Nope. No. Are you still able to walk and do the things that you used to enjoy doing? I know you said gardening was hard for you. How about like other activities of like daily living like, uhm, cooking, using the washroom, things like that? Yeah, those things I don't really have too much trouble with. No, OK, uhm, so it sounds like what you may have is something called arthritis, which basically happens, uhm, when the cushions at the ends of your bones and your joints kind of degenerate over the years, and so bone can eventually rub on bone, which causes like the grading sensation and causes the pain. Uhm, and it's it happens in old age, it happens with wear and tear so to diagnose it, we we'll, yeah, we did the physical exam. Uhm, and another thing that may be worth considering is occupational therapy because they can help you find ways to do everyday tasks without putting too much stress on an already painful joint. OK yeah, no worries.
